1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook/package code: ground truth is deterministic — terminals self-verify auth tokens and report online/total via /status, and scoring is online/total subsystems plus a token/iteration fine; no subjective judging. Verification logic is unambiguous and reproducible (fixed seeds).

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page (system.md / docstrings) claims it measures cross-run agentic learning and mastering activation of unknown nested terminals; notebook code (tasks.py run_adaptive_learning + infrastructure.run_agent_loop) genuinely sends prompts/tools to the model and scores the model's actual ability to bring subsystems ONLINE across increasingly complex chains. Tightly aligned and clearly model-measuring.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: modular package (tasks/infrastructure/terminals/server), sandboxed VFS, retry logic, both GenAI and OpenAI tool loops, telemetry, deterministic seeding, graceful exception handling around runs. Clean and maintainable.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook: 10 generators (TaskSpecGenerator tasks_count=50 plus a CompositeTaskGenerator), each producing 50 seeded terminal configurations — hundreds of distinct evaluation instances across difficulty tiers, far exceeding 20.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook computes distinct dimensions: success (subsystems online / fully-passed generators) AND efficiency (token + iteration fine subtracted from score), plus per-terminal activation-step telemetry — two genuinely different performance dimensions with rationale in system.md.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose (system.md + sparse notebook markdown) clearly names the capability — long-term agentic learning/skill-retention across runs and token efficiency — and explains the goal, but the notebook's own markdown is mostly setup notes and there is no broader research framing or gap relative to existing agentic benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Both artifacts: terminal .md spec files, prompts, docstrings on infrastructure, and the system prompt thoroughly describe protocol, scoring, persistence and reproduction; a reader can understand the evaluation without reverse-engineering, covering data flow and scoring.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page states a specific hypothesis (mastering activation of unknown nested terminal configs across runs); notebook isolates it with a deliberate difficulty gradient (T1 single → composite T1|T2|T3|T4|T5 chains) and persistent-folder learning across runs, enabling attribution of performance to the targeted skill.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook: items are explicitly stratified into 10 generators of escalating structural complexity (depth and terminal-type mixtures), and scoring reports per-generator passes and per-terminal activation steps — a structured difficulty taxonomy with per-condition reporting.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: deterministic gates (word-count band, forbidden-token regex, bracket extraction) are verifiable, but the substantive judgment relies on a 4-judge LLM senate whose criteria are 3-4 yes/no questions with no anchored score levels — a rubric exists but is not multi-dimensional with anchored examples.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims it measures metacognitive/analogical 'epistemic exile' — explaining modern concepts within an old epistemic frame; notebook does prompt the model and judge its response, but heavy formatting constraints (bracket tags, strict word band, forbidden-token list) conflate the target with instruction-following/format compliance, so failures often reflect constraint-handling rather than the claimed capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: functional and readable with regex extraction and senate aggregation, but repetitive across 5 near-duplicate notebooks, hardcoded judge model keys, and limited handling of judge failures (silently continues); rough edges remain.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook: each task runs exactly ONE prompt/scenario against the target model (single item per notebook); even taken together the 5 notebooks are 5 distinct single-item tasks, far below 10.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook computes deterministic structural pass/fail (word count, lexical) plus per-criterion senate pass ratios, but these all reduce to one underlying dimension (did the response satisfy the scenario); no second distinct dimension and no documented mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose (docstrings/descriptions) names the capability and scenario vividly ('Evaluating Epistemic Exile: Narrow AI vs AGI in Ge'ez for King Ezana') and the win/loss framing motivates it, but there is no markdown task page connecting it to a broader research question or gap, and it reads as flavor more than a research motivation.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Both artifacts: docstrings describe each scenario and the code is self-explanatory, but there is no markdown walkthrough of scoring rules, judge selection rationale, or how the senate threshold/criteria were chosen — partial documentation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page targets a clear domain (constrained analogical explanation across epistemic boundaries); notebook adds a partial control via paired language conditions (Aksum English vs Ge'ez; Bolivar English vs Spanish), but each task is a single free-form item with no within-task control conditions to isolate the mechanism, so confounds remain.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook: across the 5 notebooks there is natural variation in scenario, era, concept, and language, but each task itself contains one item with no internal sub-condition labels or difficulty tiers reported in code.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: each World-Swap problem is generated with a deterministic correct_answer and naive_answer, scored with normalization, verbose-correct guard, and multi-layer slop detection (keyword/numeric/embedding/token-overlap); structured-output schema enforces fields. Ground truth is unambiguous and robustly verified.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims a metacognition diagnostic (meta-d'/d', calibration, selective prediction, inhibition); notebook prompts the model on 600 items and computes those exact SDT/decision-theoretic measures from the model's actual answers and confidences, with Tier-1 prompt scaffolding deliberately matched to Tier-2+ to remove the scaffolding confound — tightly aligned and clearly model-measuring.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: well-sectioned, dataclasses, fixed seeds, parallel inference with caching, _safe_call error capture, protobuf sanitization, bootstrap CIs, fallback embedding/token slop layers — clean, robust, idiomatic edge-case handling.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook: generate_dataset produces N=600 items (150/tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types), vastly exceeding 20 and enabling per-tier/per-type analysis.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook computes many genuinely distinct dimensions — accuracy/d', metacognitive efficiency (meta-d'/d'), calibration (ECE, ACE Brier with asymmetric overconfidence weight), selective-prediction value, tool utility — with rationale in markdown and bootstrap CIs; multiple independent performance axes.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose (notebook header markdown) clearly names the capability (metacognition) and enumerates tasks, metrics, failure modes, and dataset size, but it is a structured spec sheet rather than a motivated narrative connecting to a research gap or why existing benchmarks fall short.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Both artifacts: extensive section-header markdown plus the task-page summary document the World-Swap engine, slop detection, SDT/calibration math, prompt design, and scoring; metric definitions and dataset construction are interpretable and reproducible (seeded).

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page states explicit hypotheses (inhibition, inverse scaling, calibration under world-swap); notebook isolates them with strong controls — identity-rule Tier 1 baseline vs swapped Tier 2-4, scaffold-equalized prompts, per-tier accuracy/confidence, and an inverse-scaling index — allowing attribution to the targeted sub-capabilities.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook: items are explicitly organized by 4 difficulty tiers x 4 problem types with labels, and compute_profile reports tier_accs/tier_confs and per-failure-mode rates — a structured taxonomy with per-condition reporting that reveals a performance gradient.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: dataset questions carry deterministic answer keys, but the model-calling tasks score with fuzzy substring/token-overlap matching against encoded answer strings (e.g. 'fast:sts8:d58:v44:8224'), and the headline staleness check is a brittle keyword test ('stale'/'old'/'outdated') — ground truth exists but verification is fragile and partly mismatched to free-form output.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims it measures LLM temporal reasoning (validity windows vs decay); the headline staleness/AsOfQA notebooks do prompt the model, but with substantial confounds (substring scoring, encoded answer formats the model cannot reproduce) and several core notebooks (temporalbench-v2-pccc, temporalbenchv4) never call the model at all — they build the authors' own TemporalAttentionStore and assert store.get(...) is not None, testing their own code. The primary eval broadly measures the model but is confounded and surrounded by self-asserting auxiliaries.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: functional with data-loading helpers and dataclasses, but uneven — a mocked staleness notebook with ~3 items, several notebooks with the model parameter unused, bare except: pass loops, and an answer-format mismatch that yields 0.0/734 in v4; rough and inconsistent across notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook/data: the dataset packages thousands of questions per version (v1_seed0 alone has 2000 questions; multiple versions/seeds), and the full-dataset tasks iterate over hundreds-to-thousands of items, far exceeding 20 (the tiny mocked staleness demo notwithstanding).

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook: the model-facing tasks compute only a single accuracy/pass-rate via substring match with no justification or second dimension, and the chosen exact/substring matching is largely inappropriate for the encoded free-form answers it compares against.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose (temporalbench-eval markdown header) names the capability (temporal reasoning: validity windows vs decay) and the track, giving basic motivation, but does not develop the research gap or why existing temporal benchmarks are insufficient beyond slogan-level claims.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Both artifacts: there are dataset schemas, task docstrings, and a results/key-findings block, but scoring rules, the encoded answer format, and how ground truth maps to model output are under-documented and the self-asserting notebooks are not explained — partial coverage.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page targets temporal reasoning with sub-families (AsOfQA, PastQueryTrap, ChangeDetection, CurrentQuery, OverlapQuery, staleness) and a 'validity windows vs decay' hypothesis; but the model-facing implementation lacks clean control vs perturbation conditions and several intended conditions don't actually probe the model, weakening attribution.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook/data: items vary across task families and versions (v1-v4, multiple seeds, fast/slow domains), but the model-facing tasks generally report only an aggregate count and the broken/self-asserting notebooks do not break results down by difficulty in a way tied to model performance.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: code-golf and regex-golf tasks verify solutions by executing model output against hidden asserts / re.fullmatch over explicit MATCH and DO_NOT_MATCH lists, and EGAT scores via deterministic embedding geometry; correctness is unambiguous and fully verifiable with pre-validated baselines.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims it measures syntactical/algorithmic compression and constraint reasoning under feedback; notebook code genuinely prompts the model over up-to-10 reprompt trials and scores the model's actual submissions by fractional character/length reduction (or embedding convergence) — implementation isolates the claimed compression-under-constraint capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: clean and consistent across the three notebooks — robust option extraction, fresh-namespace exec, traceback trimming, system-role rejection fallback with task restart, concise feedback strings, and pre-validated baselines; handles malformed responses gracefully.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook: each sub-benchmark has exactly 5 items (5 golf tasks / 5 regex sessions / 5 EGAT prompts). Each is amplified by 10 reprompting trials, but the count of distinct evaluation items per task is 5, placing it in the 10-or-fewer range — modest coverage.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook computes a well-motivated continuous metric (fractional reduction / geometric convergence, with -1.0 penalty for no valid solution) and reports per-item plus aggregate, but it is essentially one performance dimension per task with no second distinct dimension (e.g. validity rate vs efficiency) reported.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose (each notebook's markdown header) clearly names the capability (code/regex compression, embedding-geometry navigation), explains the scoring intuition and lists tasks, but does not situate the benchmark against existing evaluations or articulate the broader research gap it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Both artifacts: markdown headers describe purpose, scoring formula, per-task baselines, and config flags, while code comments document the evaluator, extraction regex, and reprompt protocol — a reader can reproduce the scoring across both task page and notebook.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page states a specific hypothesis (shortest-valid-output under hard constraints / embedding-space navigation); notebook isolates it with fixed pre-validated baselines, hidden tests / explicit accept-reject lists, and a difficulty gradient across the 5 items, allowing attribution of the score to compression skill rather than confounds.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook: items are an explicitly labeled, titled difficulty progression (e.g. baseline_chars 449->1465; regex baselines 21->56 with increasing constraint complexity; EGAT prompts 1->19 tokens) and per-item scores are reported, giving a structured gradient that reveals where models fail.
